case_studies.title: Febrile Neutropenia in a Pediatric Oncology Patient
case_studies.vignette: A 7-year-old boy undergoing induction chemotherapy for acute lymphoblastic leukemia presents to the pediatric oncology unit in a tertiary hospital in India with a fever of 38.7°C for the past 2 hours. His mother reports that he has been less active, refusing food, and complaining of mild abdominal discomfort. No diarrhea or vomiting is noted. The family lives in a rural area, 200 km from the hospital, and traveled overnight to reach the unit. On examination, the child appears lethargic but arousable, with pale conjunctiva and a heart rate of 130 bpm. His respiratory rate is 28/min, and his blood pressure is 90/60 mmHg. There is no rash, oral mucositis is grade 1, and his abdomen is soft with no organomegaly or tenderness. The central line site is clean with no signs of infection. A complete blood count reveals a white blood cell count of 0.4 x 10^9/L, hemoglobin of 7.8 g/dL, and platelets of 18,000/µL. A bedside capillary blood glucose is 82 mg/dL.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize and prioritize bedside supportive care in febrile neutropenia, including timely antibiotic administration, transfusion thresholds, and monitoring for abdominal or central line-associated complications in a resource-limited setting.

case_studies.title: Febrile Neutropenia in a Pediatric Oncology Patient
case_studies.vignette: A 7-year-old boy with acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) undergoing induction chemotherapy presents to a pediatric oncology unit in a tertiary care hospital in India. He developed fever (39.2°C) at home 4 hours ago and appears lethargic. His parents report no other symptoms but mention he has been eating poorly and has had no bowel movements for 2 days. On examination, he is febrile, pale, and tachycardic (HR 140/min), with a capillary refill time of 3 seconds. His blood pressure is 90/60 mmHg, and he has no obvious source of infection on physical examination. His central line site is clean. A complete blood count performed earlier today at a local lab showed an absolute neutrophil count (ANC) of 200 cells/mm³. The family traveled 2 hours to reach the hospital and has limited access to transportation for follow-up care. They are visibly anxious and request clarity on the next steps.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize febrile neutropenia as an oncologic emergency, prioritize timely antibiotic administration, and assess the need for admission in a resource-limited setting while addressing family concerns.

subtopics.subtopic_title: PEDIATRIC EARLY WARNING SCORES AND QUALITY IMPROVEMENT BUNDLES
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Pediatric Early Warning Scores (PEWS) are designed to detect clinical deterioration early, preventing events like cardiac arrest, late septic shock, or unplanned transfers. In pediatric oncology, PEWS trends must be interpreted alongside diagnosis-specific risks such as neutropenia, recent chemotherapy, mucositis, central line complications, tumor lysis syndrome (TLS), bleeding, steroid masking, and caregiver concerns. A normal blood pressure does not exclude shock; rising heart rate, delayed perfusion, tachypnea, new oxygen requirement, altered behavior, and reduced urine output are critical indicators.
- **Clinical application:** Escalation thresholds should be explicitly defined in writing: low PEWS scores necessitate repeat observations, moderate scores require resident review, and high scores mandate consultant and critical care team notification. Any clinician or caregiver concern should override score thresholds. Charts must specify the frequency of vital sign monitoring, personnel to be contacted, expected response times, and required interventions. Scoring without appropriate clinical response is inadequate and compromises safety.
- **Quality improvement:** Quality improvement bundles should address common high-yield failures in pediatric oncology care. Key focus areas include reducing fever door-to-needle time, ensuring central line maintenance, accurate mucositis grading, matching antiemetic orders to emetogenicity, timely response to transfusion reactions, adherence to TLS laboratory monitoring schedules, completing sedation checklists, and reinforcing discharge fever-plan teach-back. Monthly audits of delays and near misses are essential to identify and rectify system gaps.
- **Safety/follow-up:** In resource-limited settings like Indian centers with constrained ICU beds and blood product availability, preventing clinical deterioration through proactive quality improvement bundles is more feasible than relying on late-stage rescue. Regular monitoring, timely interventions, and caregiver education are critical to improving outcomes. The Indian Childhood Cancer Initiative and WHO CureAll framework provide valuable guidance for implementing these measures effectively.

case_studies.title: PEWS and Escalation in a Febrile Pediatric Oncology Patient
case_studies.vignette: A 7-year-old boy undergoing treatment for acute lymphoblastic leukemia presents to the pediatric oncology ward in a tertiary care hospital in India. He is on Day 5 post-chemotherapy and has been febrile for the past 6 hours. His mother reports reduced activity, poor oral intake, and decreased urine output over the last 12 hours. On examination, his heart rate is 142/min, respiratory rate is 32/min, oxygen saturation is 94% on room air, and his capillary refill time is 4 seconds. He appears pale and mildly irritable but responds to verbal stimuli. His PEWS score is 6. The nurse notes that his peripheral pulses are weak and his extremities are cool. No obvious source of infection is identified. A caregiver expresses concern that he is 'not like himself.' The team is notified, but there is a delay in the resident's arrival due to a concurrent emergency. The patient’s condition is reassessed 30 minutes later, with no improvement in vital signs.
case_studies.learning_objective: To identify and appropriately escalate care in a pediatric oncology patient with clinical signs of deterioration and an elevated PEWS score, integrating caregiver concerns and system-level response protocols.

case_studies.title: Early Detection of Clinical Deterioration in a Febrile Pediatric Oncology Patient
case_studies.vignette: A 6-year-old boy with acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) undergoing induction chemotherapy presents to the pediatric oncology ward in a tertiary care hospital in India with a 2-day history of fever and fatigue. His mother reports reduced oral intake and two episodes of vomiting. On admission, his vitals are heart rate 140/min, respiratory rate 34/min, temperature 38.5°C, and capillary refill time 3 seconds. He appears lethargic but responds to verbal stimuli. Examination reveals pallor, dry mucous membranes, and no obvious source of infection. PEWS is calculated as 5. The nurse notes his urine output has decreased over the past 6 hours, and his mother expresses concern that he is 'not himself.' A central line is in place but shows no signs of infection. His hemoglobin is 7.8 g/dL, and absolute neutrophil count (ANC) is 200/µL. Despite antipyretics, his heart rate remains elevated, and his perfusion worsens over the next hour.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize the importance of Pediatric Early Warning Scores (PEWS) and caregiver concerns in identifying clinical deterioration in pediatric oncology patients, and understand the escalation thresholds and response protocols in resource-limited settings.

case_studies.title: Pediatric Early Warning Scores in a Febrile Oncology Patient
case_studies.vignette: A 6-year-old boy with acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) on maintenance chemotherapy presents to a district hospital in rural India with a 2-day history of fever and fatigue. His mother reports reduced oral intake and urine output over the past 24 hours. On arrival, his heart rate is 150 bpm, respiratory rate is 36/min, oxygen saturation is 94% on room air, and capillary refill time is 4 seconds. He appears pale and lethargic but responds to verbal stimuli. A central venous catheter is in place, and the site appears clean. The nurse calculates a Pediatric Early Warning Score (PEWS) of 5 due to tachycardia, delayed capillary refill, and altered behavior. The attending resident is called to assess the child. The mother expresses concern, stating, 'He’s not like this at home.'
case_studies.learning_objective: To apply Pediatric Early Warning Scores (PEWS) to identify clinical deterioration in a febrile pediatric oncology patient and recognize the need for timely escalation of care.

case_studies.title: Pediatric Early Warning Scores in an Oncology Patient with Persistent Fever
case_studies.vignette: A 7-year-old boy with acute lymphoblastic leukemia, currently in the induction phase of chemotherapy, presents to the pediatric oncology ward in a tertiary care hospital in India with a 24-hour history of fever and lethargy. He received his last dose of chemotherapy three days ago. His mother reports reduced oral intake and decreased urine output over the past 12 hours. On initial assessment, his heart rate is 140/min, respiratory rate is 36/min, blood pressure is 90/60 mmHg, and oxygen saturation is 94% on room air. Capillary refill time is 4 seconds. He appears pale and mildly irritable but is arousable. The central line site shows no visible redness or discharge. A Pediatric Early Warning Score (PEWS) of 6 is documented. The resident notes caregiver concern about his worsening condition and requests a consultant review. No recent laboratory results are available at the time of assessment.
case_studies.learning_objective: To evaluate the application of Pediatric Early Warning Scores and escalation thresholds in the context of pediatric oncology, emphasizing early recognition of clinical deterioration and appropriate response protocols.
